g.data-narration: Welcome to Module 3: Understanding the Blockchain Transaction Life Cycle
In centralized systems like banks, a single authority updates a ledger. Blockchain is fundamentally different - it's decentralized.
g.text: Traditional banking: One central authority updates a ledger.
trust through consensus without a central authority.

g.data-narration: A blockchain transaction travels through 7 key stages from creation to final confirmation on the distributed ledger.
g.text: Create → Sign → Broadcast → Mempool → Mine → Confirm → Finality
Each stage adds security through cryptographic proof and distributed agreement.

g.data-narration: Alice creates a transaction with inputs (previous tx refs), outputs (Bob's address + amount), and signs it with her private key.
The signed transaction is broadcast to all connected nodes via P2P gossip protocol, propagating across the network in seconds.
Every node validates the transaction: verifies the digital signature and checks for double-spending using its blockchain copy.
Valid transactions enter the mempool - a waiting area of unconfirmed transactions. Each node maintains its own local mempool.
g.text: • Each node has its own local mempool
• Not global - may differ between nodes

g.data-narration: Miners select transactions from the mempool, prioritize by fee rate, and perform Proof-of-Work to create a new block.
The miner broadcasts the valid block. Other nodes verify PoW and transactions, then add it to their blockchain - achieving consensus.
Each subsequent block adds a confirmation, making reversal exponentially harder. 6 confirmations (~1 hour) is considered final.
The complete transaction lifecycle moves from user intent to network-verified fact through 7 stages, achieving decentralization and trust.
g.text: Create → Sign → Broadcast → Validate → Mempool → Mine → Confirm → Finality
